straight_beat.name: Winston Delacroix
straight_beat.column:
The Marlins’ bats woke up like a pack of wolves last night, and the Braves’ bullpen didn’t have the wherewithal to keep them at bay. Ten runs, four innings, and a record that’s now 10-7, three games back in the NL East. That’s how the ledger reads.  

You could argue the Braves’ offense had its moments—Acuna’s swing, Strider’s slider, the way they’ve carved up opponents in the past. But last night, the Marlins’ lineup looked like a well-oiled machine, pounding the Braves’ defense with line drives and home runs. Miami’s offense didn’t just dominate; it felt like they’d studied the Braves’ defensive alignments and found a weakness in every corner. The Braves’ middle infielders, usually reliable, looked like they’d been handed a playbook in a language they didn’t speak.  

And then there’s the bullpen. The same unit that’s carried the Braves through so many tight games this season seemed to forget how to close. The Marlins’ relief corps didn’t just hold serve—they turned the

optimist.name: Caledonia 'Callie' Peach
optimist.column: The Braves lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour our sweet tea. A 10-4 defeat to the Marlins is a bitter cup, sure—but I’ve got a hunch this team’s got the grit to turn that bitterness into a sweet swing. You know what I’m talking about, right? pessimist.name: Earl Tidwell
pessimist.column:
The Braves lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to rip your hair out. Ten to four. A game that should’ve been a coronation for the pitching staff, a validation of the front office’s winter moves, a chance to finally put the “winning” in “Braves.” Instead, it’s another chapter in the long, sputtering narrative of a team that seems to have forgotten how to win.  

Let’s start with the pitching. The starters, for all their supposed upgrades, looked like a group of guys playing dress-up in a baseball uniform. Max Fried, who’s been the ace of this whole rebuild, lasted just four innings. Four! And not four innings of dominance, either—four innings of “I’m trying, but I’m not trying hard enough.” He gave up four runs, including a home run to a guy who’s probably been on a hot streak for the last three weeks. You know who that is? The Marlins’ leadoff hitter. The one who’s been hitting .320 since the All-Star break. Fried’s line: 68 pitches, four runs, a walk, and a strikeout. Not the kind of numbers you want when you’re trying to build a playoff rotation.  

And then there’s the bullpen. Oh, the bullpen. The one that’s been the subject of so many “they’re getting better” headlines. The one that’s been the subject of so many “they’re not getting better” headlines. Raisel Iglesias, who’s been a free agent for a year now, came in to close the game and gave up three runs in
